ontology.foodex2_description: Enchiladas — tortilla-based baked dish with filling and sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for chicken enchiladas; A0CKN (composite dish — tortilla-based) used as closest defensible anchor. Chicken enchiladas are a tortilla-wrapped baked composite dish consistent with this category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for enchiladas or Mexican composite baked dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of chicken enchiladas is 2 pieces (approximately 450 g) filled with seasoned chicken, smothered in enchilada sauce, and topped with melted cheese.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Enchilada, chicken, with sauce and cheese (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 151 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 8.44 g, carbohydrate 8.89 g) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for chicken enchiladas with sauce and cheese (FDC ID 173700 and related entries). Values retained from input and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy composite for chicken enchilada. Secondary micronutrient values (calcium 88.89 mg, iron 0.78 mg, cholesterol 33.33 mg, sodium 266.67 mg, vitamin C 1.11 mg, saturated fat 4.0 g, trans fat 0.11 g, dietary fibre 1.11 g, sugar 1.33 g) are consistent with USDA data range for this dish type. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and other micronutrients set to -1 where not reported in the input and not directly available from a single USDA entry for this composite.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken enchiladas as a composite dish. GI estimated at 52 (low-medium boundary) based on the primary carbohydrate carrier: corn tortillas (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database, tortilla corn entry). The enchilada sauce, cheese, and chicken filling contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 52 × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 40.0 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 = 20.8, rounded to 20.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 266.67 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium enchilada sauce and limit portion to one enchilada (approximately 225 g) to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal; home preparation with low-sodium sauce and reduced cheese is strongly preferred.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 8.89 g/100 g with GI ~52 (corn tortilla base); a 450 g serving provides approximately 40 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~21.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit to one enchilada (approximately 225 g, ~20 g carbohydrate, GL ~10) per meal, pair with non-starchy vegetables to reduce glycemic impact, and choose corn over flour tortillas for a lower GI response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g and sodium 266.67 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers 18 g saturated fat (~90% of the 20 g daily limit) and 1200 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to one enchilada, request reduced-fat cheese or reduced cheese quantity, and use a homemade tomato-based enchilada sauce without added salt to manage both saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains nightshade-derived ingredients (tomato, chili pepper) which may aggravate inflammation in nightshade-sensitive individuals; saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g is below the 5 g/100 g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and known nightshade sensitivity should avoid or limit this dish; those without nightshade sensitivity may consume in moderation, requesting reduced cheese to keep saturated fat intake low.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken enchiladas are a classic Mexican baked dish consisting of corn or flour tortillas filled with seasoned, shredded or diced chicken breast, rolled and arranged in a baking dish, then smothered in a rich red enchilada sauce made from dried chili peppers, tomato paste, garlic, cumin, and chili powder, and baked under a generous layer of melted cheddar and Monterey Jack cheese. The result is a hearty, savoury dish with a tender, slightly chewy tortilla exterior, moist and spiced chicken interior, tangy-smoky sauce, and a golden, bubbly cheese crust. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 151 kcal, 10 g protein, 8.4 g fat (4.0 g saturated), 8.9 g carbohydrate, 1.3 g sugar, 1.1 g dietary fibre, and 267 mg sodium. Calcium is notably present at 89 mg/100 g from the cheese component. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low-medium, corn tortilla basis), with a glycemic load of approximately 21 per standard 450 g serving. Clinically, the primary concerns are moderate sodium content (approximately 1200 mg per 450 g serving) relevant to hypertension management, moderate glycemic load relevant to type 2 diabetes portion control, and high saturated fat per serving relevant to cardiovascular disease risk. The dish is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians, contains gluten and dairy allergens, and may not be halal unless prepared with certified halal chicken. Nightshade-sensitive individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution due to tomato and chili pepper content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken enchiladas?
Is chicken enchilada high in saturated fat?

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for chicken enchiladas with sauce and cheese. Core macros and key micronutrients cross-validate within expected ranges for this dish type. Confidence is medium rather than high because the original source is a delivery platform with AI-generated provenance, not a direct primary laboratory analysis; USDA data used as validation anchor rather than exact match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite chicken enchilada preparation are not directly available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; individual component data would require recipe-level disaggregation beyond the scope of this record. Input ethnic_tags contained 'Naan' and 'Burrito' which are inconsistent with chicken enchiladas; these have been removed. country_of_origin in input was was listed as 'Singapore' which is inconsistent with the dish's Mexican origin; corrected to Mexico. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on corn tortilla GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chicken enchiladas as a composite dish was found in approved sources. All core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 8.44, carbohydrate_g 8.89) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for this dish type; no zero-macro justification required.
